title: Research Obsidian.md live autocomplete options

summary.bottom_line: Yes, true Obsidian live ghost-text autocomplete with Tab accept exists, but the practical GPT/Claude-subscription story is weak: true inline plugins mostly use API keys, OpenAI-compatible endpoints, or local Ollama/LM Studio; subscription/account integrations found are chat/agent/transform tools, not live tab autocomplete.
summary.notable_absence: No ready Obsidian plugin was found that provides true live inline Tab autocomplete using an existing Claude Pro/Max or ChatGPT Plus/Pro subscription in a clearly official/supported way.
summary.scope_note: No runtime installation tests were performed; mode classification is based on official listings, READMEs/manifests, GitHub status, and selected source-code inspection for keymaps/CodeMirror extension use.

mode_taxonomy.true_inline_tab: Ghost/transparent text appears inline while typing and can be accepted with Tab or configured accept key.
mode_taxonomy.inline_not_tab: Inline/ghost UI exists but Tab/insertion behavior is absent or not evidenced.
mode_taxonomy.completion_popup: Suggestions are primarily in a popup/list menu.
mode_taxonomy.command_or_selection_generation: User invokes command, slash command, prompt, tag trigger, or selection action to generate/transform text.
mode_taxonomy.custom_only: Feasible only through custom development or as a bridge/context layer.

sources.title: Editor extensions - Obsidian Developer Documentation
sources.quoted_evidence: Docs say Obsidian uses CodeMirror 6 to power the Markdown editor, and that an Obsidian editor extension is the same thing as a CodeMirror extension; examples use registerEditorExtension in plugin onload.

sources.title: Plugin.registerEditorExtension() - Obsidian Developer Documentation
sources.quoted_evidence: API docs: registerEditorExtension registers a CodeMirror 6 extension; the extension parameter must be a CodeMirror 6 Extension or array of Extensions.

sources.title: Decorations - Obsidian Developer Documentation
sources.quoted_evidence: Docs: decorations let plugins change the editor appearance; widget decorations insert elements in the document.

sources.title: EditorSuggest - Obsidian Developer Documentation
sources.quoted_evidence: API docs define EditorSuggest as extending PopoverSuggest and include getSuggestions, onTrigger, renderSuggestion, and selectSuggestion methods.

claims.text: True Copilot-like live inline/tab autocomplete exists in Obsidian community plugins, but it is mostly implemented by small or aging third-party plugins rather than a single official/standard Obsidian AI autocomplete surface.
claims.notes: Each cited README/source inspection evidences ghost/transparent inline suggestions and Tab acceptance or configurable accept key.

claims.text: For a GPT/OpenAI-compatible/local-model Copilot replacement in Obsidian, the best ready-made candidates are AI Autocomplete for low-friction OpenAI-compatible/Groq setup, Inscribe for a current local/API option, and Copilot Auto Completion/Companion/Markpilot if older mature plugins are acceptable.
claims.notes: Recommendation balances mode fit, maintenance, provider coverage, adoption, and setup complexity; not independently runtime-tested inside Obsidian.

claims.text: GitHub Copilot for Obsidian is a strong true-inline baseline but is not a replacement for GitHub Copilot because it depends on the GitHub Copilot service/subscription.
claims.notes: Useful to understand achievable UX, poor fit for replacing Copilot with GPT/Claude subscriptions.

claims.text: Options that claim direct ChatGPT/Claude/Claude Code subscription/account access in Obsidian are chat, agent, transform, or context-bridge tools, not true live inline/tab autocomplete; Smart Composer also warns that Claude subscription OAuth via third-party clients has account restriction risk.
claims.notes: This does not decide provider terms globally; it records what these plugin sources claim and the lack of inline autocomplete evidence.

claims.text: Obsidian plugin APIs make custom inline/ghost text technically feasible through CodeMirror 6 editor extensions and decorations, but there is no simple official AI-autocomplete API that removes the need to handle UI, keybindings, latency, cancellation, and provider integration.
claims.notes: Official docs prove editor extension/decorations capabilities; complexity inference comes from plugin implementations and keymap/source checks.

claims.text: Mature non-AI Obsidian completion plugins such as Various Complements, Completr, and Text Autocomplete are useful closest equivalents but are completion-popup/deterministic tools, not GPT/Claude/local LLM autocomplete.
claims.notes: They may satisfy typing-speed goals but not semantic AI autocomplete.

claims.text: Privacy and cost for Obsidian AI autocomplete are provider-dependent: remote API modes send cursor/selection/note context and bill per provider usage; local Ollama/LM Studio modes improve privacy but require local setup and may trade off latency/quality.
claims.notes: Several plugin docs directly distinguish local Ollama from remote API providers.

options.name: AI Autocomplete
options.readme_quote: README: AI inline writing completion for Obsidian, powered by OpenAI-compatible APIs; ghost text suggestions appear inline and Tab accepts, Esc dismisses; defaults route through OpenRouter to Groq for fast inference.
evidence: Official listing says inline AI writing completion with ghost text, powered by Groq, Tab to accept.
README says it uses OpenAI-compatible APIs, default OpenRouter/Groq, 800ms trigger delay, and Tab/Esc controls.
Source code check found a CodeMirror keymap binding Tab to insert the inline suggestion and Escape to clear it.
strengths: Closest ready-made GPT/OpenAI-compatible Copilot-like UX found: live ghost text plus Tab accept.
Simple setup for OpenRouter/Groq or any OpenAI-compatible chat-completions endpoint.
Works on desktop and mobile per manifest/listing.
limitations: Very new and low adoption; official stats show only low hundreds of downloads.
Default model/provider is OpenRouter/Groq, not Claude or a ChatGPT/Claude UI subscription.
Quality depends on chat-completion prompting rather than a specialized code/text FIM model unless user supplies one.
options.privacy_posture: Remote API by default; vault context around the cursor is sent to the configured OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Privacy improves only with a local compatible endpoint.
options.cost_model: Bring an OpenRouter/Groq/OpenAI-compatible API key; usage is API-billed or provider-metered, not covered by ChatGPT/Claude UI subscriptions.
ux_gaps: No evidence of VS Code-grade multiline/FIM tuning, telemetry, model selection, or mature conflict handling.
Small project maturity increases breakage risk.

access_model.access_notes: Uses API-key style OpenAI-compatible endpoints; no consumer subscription reuse evidenced.

plugin_api_and_editor_mode_constraints.finding: Obsidian Live Preview is CodeMirror 6; true ghost-text plugins use editor extensions/decorations/keymaps rather than a simple official AI-completion API.
Completion popups are supported through EditorSuggest/PopoverSuggest-style APIs, but that is a different UX from VS Code Copilot ghost text.
Tab can conflict with other Obsidian plugins/keybindings; Markpilot and GitHub Copilot plugin READMEs both discuss Tab/keybinding issues.
Mobile support varies: some inline options are desktop-only, while AI Autocomplete, Companion, and Thought Completion list non-desktop-only support.

privacy_and_cost_summary: True inline GPT-like plugins mostly need API keys or local endpoints; consumer ChatGPT/Claude subscriptions should not be assumed to cover API calls.
Local Ollama/LM Studio paths are the strongest privacy/cost-control path but require local model setup and may have latency/quality tradeoffs.
Subscription/account-connection plugins found for Smart Composer, Claude Code wrappers, and Chatting with AI are not live autocomplete and may carry account/policy or tool-permission risks.
